definition: Family name of the first author, used as part of the citation handle.
reviewer_question: Who is the first author?
categories_or_examples: Examples: Shahn; Carvalho

definition: Year of publication, recorded separately from citation to support time-trend analysis.
reviewer_question: What year was this published?

definition: The healthcare context from which patient data was drawn, defined strictly by where care occurred (not by disease).
reviewer_question: In what care setting were these patients observed?
categories_or_examples: ICU/critical care; inpatient (non-ICU); outpatient/ambulatory; emergency department; long-term care/SNF; home/wearable monitoring; multi-setting; unclear/not stated

definition: The clinical specialty or disease area the study targets, independent of care setting.
reviewer_question: What disease area or clinical specialty does this study address?
categories_or_examples: cardiology; neurology; nephrology; oncology; critical care medicine; infectious disease; psychiatry; endocrinology; multiple chronic conditions; not disease-specific; other

definition: The epidemiological framing of the cohort.
reviewer_question: Is this a retrospective cohort, case-control, prospective cohort, cross-sectional, or other design?
categories_or_examples: retrospective cohort; case-control; prospective cohort; cross-sectional; nested case-control; other; not stated

definition: The temporal length of each patient's record actually used in modeling.
reviewer_question: Across how long a window was each patient observed in the data fed to the model?
categories_or_examples: Examples: '1 year pre + 1 year post index'; 'first 48 h of ICU stay'; 'median 4.2 yr (IQR 2.1–7.0)'

definition: Wall-clock years spanned by the source data.
reviewer_question: What calendar years does the source data cover?

definition: Named primary EHR dataset, registry, or extracted database used for modeling.
reviewer_question: What is the named primary dataset or datasets?
categories_or_examples: Examples: MIMIC-III; MIMIC-IV; eICU; MarketScan Medicaid; UK Biobank; institutional EHR

definition: Non-EHR knowledge bases, ontologies as data, or external databases used to enrich the graph or model.
reviewer_question: Did the study use any auxiliary knowledge sources beyond the primary EHR?
categories_or_examples: TWOSIDES (DDI); DrugBank; SNOMED-CT (as KG, not coding); ATC hierarchy; UMLS; ICD as KG; ChEMBL; MedDRA; none; other

definition: Hospitals, health systems, or administrative sources from which data physically originated.
reviewer_question: Where did the patients physically receive care? Single center, multi-center, or administrative pool?
categories_or_examples: single-center (specify); multi-center within one system; multi-system; national administrative claims; mixed; unclear

definition: The unit on which sample size is reported and on which the model operates.
reviewer_question: What is the analytic unit: patients, admissions, ICU stays, visits, or events?
categories_or_examples: patients; hospital admissions; ICU stays; encounters/visits; events; mixed (specify); not clearly stated

definition: Total number of analytic units in the final cohort, in the unit specified by 'Unit of analysis'.
reviewer_question: How many units (patients/admissions/etc.) are in the final analytic dataset?

definition: Sample sizes for any reported subdivisions: train/validation/test, case/control, exposed/unexposed, derivation/external, or per-dataset.
reviewer_question: What sub-cohort sizes does the paper report?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'train 9,000 / val 1,500 / test 2,081'; '1,850 cases / 10,731 controls'; 'MIMIC-III 7,493; MIMIC-IV 10,000'

definition: Demographics as the authors report them: sex/gender, age, race/ethnicity, comorbidity profile, geography, socioeconomic indicators.
reviewer_question: How do the authors describe the population?
categories_or_examples: Example: 'Mean age 64 ± 12, 53% female, 67% White, 22% Black, 11% Hispanic'

definition: The author-stated rules defining who entered the cohort.
reviewer_question: What inclusion and exclusion rules did the authors apply?
categories_or_examples: Example: 'Adult afib patients with ≥1 yr observation pre and post index; excluded if left Medicaid <1 yr after index'

definition: The paper's own statement of its aim, captured verbatim or near-verbatim.
reviewer_question: How do the authors state the objective of this study?
categories_or_examples: Example: 'To predict strokes in patients with prior diagnoses of atrial fibrillation by incorporating temporal relations among health events'

definition: The ML formulation of the modeling problem, expressed in computer-science terms.
reviewer_question: Computationally, what kind of task is this?
categories_or_examples: binary classification; multiclass classification; multilabel classification; regression; time-to-event/survival; sequence-to-sequence/generation; representation learning/embedding; clustering; anomaly detection; other

definition: The specific clinical phenomenon being predicted, classified, or characterized. Decompose into outcome label, horizon, and threshold if applicable.
reviewer_question: What clinical event, state, or label is the model targeting?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'Stroke within 1 yr of afib index'; 'ICU mortality within 24 h'; '30-day readmission'; 'next-visit diagnosis codes'; 'sepsis onset within 6 h'

definition: High-level grouping of the clinical use case.
reviewer_question: Which broad category of clinical task does this fall into?
categories_or_examples: diagnosis/disease prediction; deterioration/mortality prediction; medication recommendation; readmission prediction; length-of-stay prediction; disease progression/trajectory forecasting; phenotyping/clustering; patient similarity/retrieval; treatment effect/causal inference; risk stratification (general); other

definition: The temporal distance between prediction time and target event.
reviewer_question: How far ahead is the model predicting?
categories_or_examples: Examples: '1 year'; 'next 6 hours'; 'at next visit'; 'concurrent / no horizon'; 'variable per patient'

definition: High-level categories of clinical data used as model input.
reviewer_question: Which broad data types feed the model?
categories_or_examples: diagnoses/conditions; medications; laboratory values; vitals/physiological signals; waveform signals; therapeutic interventions; procedures; demographics; clinical notes (text); discharge summaries; imaging; genomics/omics; provider/workflow; insurance/billing; social determinants; other

definition: Verbatim or near-verbatim list of specific features/variables used, with their representation.
reviewer_question: What specific variables did the authors actually use?
categories_or_examples: Example: 'ICD-9 codes mapped to OMOP concept hierarchy + drug eras; SpO2; warfarin exposure flag; 100,000+ binary predictors plus age and gender'

definition: Coding system or ontology applied to structured features as part of data preparation.
reviewer_question: Did the authors use a named coding standard or ontology to encode features?
categories_or_examples: ICD-9; ICD-10; PheCodes; SNOMED-CT; RxNorm; LOINC; OMOP CDM; UMLS; ATC; CCS; CPT; none/raw text; other

definition: Numerical representation format of features as input to the model.
reviewer_question: How are features numerically represented as model inputs?
categories_or_examples: one-hot; multi-hot; dense embedding; continuous values; mixed; not stated

definition: Whether embeddings/representations are learned from scratch, transferred from pretrained models, or LLM-generated.
reviewer_question: How are embeddings initialized?
categories_or_examples: random/from scratch; Word2Vec or skip-gram; SPPMI-SVD; Bio-Clinical BERT/ClinicalBERT; general BERT; LLM-generated (e.g., GPT-4); pretrained vision model (e.g., DenseNet); domain-pretrained on EHR; not applicable; other

definition: The mathematical class of the graph data structure.
reviewer_question: What type of graph is this mathematically?
categories_or_examples: simple graph; multigraph; hypergraph; heterogeneous graph; knowledge graph; bipartite graph; tree/hierarchy; other

definition: The formal temporal-graph paradigm to which the study's graph(s) correspond.
reviewer_question: Which dynamic-graph type(s) does this study instantiate?
categories_or_examples: DTDG (discrete-time dynamic graph); CTDG (continuous-time dynamic graph); Unrolled temporal graph; Static-with-time-outside-graph (should be excluded by inclusion criteria); Hybrid; Unclear/not stated

definition: Which aspect(s) of the graph actually change over time.
reviewer_question: Across time, what changes — node set, edge set, node attributes, edge attributes, or some combination?
categories_or_examples: nodes added/removed; edges added/removed; node attributes update; edge attributes/weights update; topology static, only attributes change; nothing changes (static); other

definition: Per-patient temporal structure of graph instances.
reviewer_question: How many graph instances does each patient contribute?
categories_or_examples: one graph per patient (unrolled); sequence of snapshots per patient; continuous stream of events per patient; one shared graph across all patients with patient-specific attributes; multiple per-patient graphs (e.g., per visit); other

definition: High-level summary of the philosophy used to build the graph(s), distinct from node and edge mechanics individually.
reviewer_question: At a high level, how was the graph built?
categories_or_examples: co-occurrence-based; ontology-/knowledge-derived; similarity-based (kNN/correlation); learned end-to-end; rule-based clinical mechanism; hybrid; other

definition: What real-world thing each node represents.
reviewer_question: What is a node?
categories_or_examples: clinical concept (diagnosis/medication/procedure/lab); patient; visit/encounter/admission; ICU stay; time point/window; provider/clinician; ward/location; medical event instance; ontology class/hierarchy concept; drug atom/molecular substructure; physiological variable; other

definition: Data attached to each node beyond its identity.
reviewer_question: What information is stored on each node?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'ICD code embedding'; 'demographic vector'; 'time-stamped lab values'; 'one-hot identity only'; 'fuzzy membership'

definition: How nodes were instantiated as objects in the graph.
reviewer_question: How were nodes created?
categories_or_examples: direct one-to-one with raw data items; ontology/hierarchy-derived; clustered/grouped from raw events; pre-trained embedding; learned end-to-end; manually defined; other

definition: Whether/how clinical or ontological knowledge guided node definition.
reviewer_question: Did the authors use medical ontology, clinician input, or hierarchy to define nodes?
categories_or_examples: yes — explicit ontology (named); yes — clinician guidance; yes — published taxonomy; no — purely data-driven; not stated

definition: The clinical/computational meaning of edges, aligned with manuscript §4.2's five-category taxonomy.
reviewer_question: What kind of relationship does an edge represent?
categories_or_examples: temporal/chronological; similarity/co-occurrence; encounter-membership (patient–visit, visit–code); knowledge/hierarchy — direct (ontology 'is-a'); knowledge/hierarchy — indirect (shared parent); learned dependency/influence/attention-derived; causal/mechanistic; other

definition: Data attached to edges, including weights, types, and time stamps.
reviewer_question: What information sits on each edge?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'time interval in days'; 'co-occurrence frequency'; 'attention weight'; 'edge type label'; 'binary present/absent'; 'NPMI weight'

definition: How edges were generated as objects in the graph.
reviewer_question: How were edges actually created?
categories_or_examples: manually rule-defined; deterministic computation on data (kNN, correlation, co-occurrence, NPMI, threshold); learned during training (attention, soft-adjacency, structure learning); ontology-derived; pre-existing knowledge graph; other

definition: Which clinical-temporal CONCEPT the model captures, per manuscript §4.1's taxonomy.
reviewer_question: What kind of clinical time does the model represent?
categories_or_examples: sequence (event ordering); interval (elapsed time); persistence (duration of state); trajectory (state evolution); temporal context (setting-specific time meaning)

definition: Operational way time was used in the model, per §4.1's second taxonomy.
reviewer_question: Operationally, how is time used in the model?
categories_or_examples: time interval / duration / horizon; order of events; changing influence over time; trajectory / state transition; multi-scale temporal aggregation

definition: The actual time resolution(s) at which observations or events were represented in the model.
reviewer_question: At what resolution(s) is time encoded?
categories_or_examples: sub-hourly; hourly; daily; weekly; monthly; yearly; visit-based; admission-based; irregular continuous; mixed multi-scale

definition: How real-world time was discretized or preserved.
reviewer_question: Was real time preserved, binned, or replaced by visit order?
categories_or_examples: continuous time preserved; explicit periodic binning/segmentation; visit/admission order as time surrogate; exact irregular timestamps preserved without binning; interval weighting only; fixed window from index date (e.g., first 24h/48h); mixed

definition: Where in the graph time-information actually lives.
reviewer_question: Where does time appear — as a node, node attribute, edge, edge attribute/weight, topology change, or outside the graph?
categories_or_examples: node entity (time-as-node); node attribute; edge entity (temporal edge); edge attribute / weight; graph topology change across snapshots; in model dynamics outside the graph; not encoded in graph

definition: Model-internal mechanism that processes time, separate from where time sits in the graph.
reviewer_question: What architectural component handles temporal dynamics?
categories_or_examples: RNN/LSTM/GRU over snapshots; Transformer/temporal attention; Time2Vec or similar time encoding; Neural ODE/continuous-time dynamics; temporal convolution; positional encoding; recurrence on edges; point process / intensity function; none (graph-only); other

definition: How the model handles unevenly spaced observations.
reviewer_question: How does the model deal with irregular time intervals?
categories_or_examples: imputation to regular grid; LOCF/carry-forward; explicit interval encoding; irregular-time-aware module (e.g., GRU-D); discretization to bins; not addressed; not applicable

definition: The author-given name of the proposed method.
reviewer_question: What does the paper call its model?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'RRF'; 'Med-GAT'; 'Dipole'; 'T-GCN'; 'KGDNet'; 'StructCare'

definition: Class of graph model used.
reviewer_question: Which graph-learning family does this belong to?
categories_or_examples: GCN; GAT; GraphSAGE; Graph Transformer; MPNN (generic); Temporal GNN (TGN, EvolveGCN, DyRep, etc.); GNN+RNN hybrid; Hypergraph NN; Graph autoencoder; R-GCN/relational GCN; Random forest with relational features; Probabilistic graphical model; Knowledge-graph embedding (TransE, etc.); other

definition: Other architectural pieces around or downstream of the graph component.
reviewer_question: What non-graph components are integrated?
categories_or_examples: RNN/LSTM/GRU; Transformer; CNN; MLP head; survival head (Cox-PH); attention pooling; contrastive learning module; gating mechanism; LLM module; none; other

definition: Free-text description of the complete pipeline as the paper presents it.
reviewer_question: How does the paper describe the full pipeline?
categories_or_examples: Example: 'DA-KNN graph constructor + ST-CNN + LT-GRU + GraphTransformer + SHAP-based interpretability module'

definition: How the model was evaluated within the source data.
reviewer_question: How was the model validated internally?
categories_or_examples: k-fold cross-validation (specify k); single train/val/test split; bootstrap; leave-one-out; temporal split (train early, test later); not stated

definition: Whether train/test splits were performed at the patient level (no patient appears in both train and test) or at the visit/episode level (label leakage risk).
reviewer_question: Was the train/test split done at the patient level or at the visit/episode level?
categories_or_examples: patient-level (no leakage); visit/episode-level (potential leakage); not stated; not applicable

definition: Whether the model was evaluated on data from a different institution, time period, or population.
reviewer_question: Was external validation performed?
categories_or_examples: none (internal only); temporal external (later years, same site); geographic external (different site); out-of-distribution / domain-shift (different disease/task); both temporal and geographic; not applicable

definition: Broader narrative of how authors argue (or don't argue) for generalizability.
reviewer_question: How do the authors address generalizability beyond the source data?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'Multi-site CV across 3 hospitals'; 'single-site, no external test'; 'evaluated on held-out time window only'

definition: High-level approach to model explanation.
reviewer_question: What kind of interpretability (if any) does the paper report?
categories_or_examples: none — black box (could have but didn't); not applicable (study not focused on prediction/explanation); not reported (silent on interpretability); intrinsic only (model inherently interpretable); post hoc only (explanations applied to black box); both intrinsic and post hoc

definition: Named techniques used for explanation.
reviewer_question: What specific interpretability methods are used?
categories_or_examples: attention weights/heatmaps; SHAP; GradientSHAP; LIME; gradient/saliency; integrated gradients; GNNExplainer; t-SNE/UMAP visualization; network/graph visualization (qualitative); case visualization; pseudo-attention with gradient-based importance; feature importance from tree ensemble; temporal phenotypes; factor function tables; fuzzy/symbolic rules; reconstruction coefficients; LLM-generated explanation; consistency analysis; other; none

definition: Form in which the model's learned representation appears to a downstream user.
reviewer_question: What does the model ultimately produce as its representation of a patient?
categories_or_examples: latent embedding/vector; calibrated risk score; temporal phenotype/discrete cluster; factor table/decomposition; fuzzy or symbolic rule; explicit decision tree/graph path; calculator-based score; other

definition: Whether interpretability outputs are clinically narrative (trajectory description, phenotype label) versus purely numeric attribution.
reviewer_question: Does interpretability produce a clinically narrative explanation?
categories_or_examples: yes; partial; no; not applicable

definition: Strengths/contributions as stated by authors themselves.
reviewer_question: What do the authors claim as their key advantages?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'Outperforms CHADS2 on AUC'; 'Identifies low-risk subgroup that other methods cannot'; 'First model to leverage X for Y'

definition: Categorical tags applied to author-reported strengths using a controlled vocabulary.
reviewer_question: Which standard strength categories do the authors mention?
categories_or_examples: performance superiority; novel architecture; interpretability; generalizability; robustness to sparsity/noise; clinical applicability; computational efficiency; first-of-kind contribution; multi-task capability; other

definition: Limitations explicitly acknowledged in the paper.
reviewer_question: What limitations do the authors themselves acknowledge?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'Selection bias from Medicaid exit'; 'Left-censoring of history variables'; 'Single-center data'

definition: Categorical tags applied to author-reported limitations using a controlled vocabulary.
reviewer_question: Which standard limitation categories do the authors mention?
categories_or_examples: single-center/limited generalizability; no external validation; selection bias; left-censoring of history; right-censoring of follow-up; coarse temporal binning; restricted modalities; computational burden; class imbalance; ontology dependence; retrospective documentation bias; small sample; missing data; lack of clinician validation; weak baseline comparison; no calibration assessment; label leakage risk; cross-setting discontinuity; static graph after construction; first-visit/cold-start problem; not-deployment-ready; scalability concerns; irregular sampling not handled; causal interpretation not justified; other; none reported

definition: Reviewer judgment of how necessary temporal modeling is to the clinical task, per manuscript §4.4.
reviewer_question: Is temporal modeling intrinsically required for this task, helpful but not essential, or merely supportive?
categories_or_examples: time-essential (task is intrinsically temporal); helpful but not indispensable; supportive only (time is an indexing device); unclear

definition: Free-text reviewer notes capturing anything unusual that doesn't fit elsewhere.
reviewer_question: Anything else worth noting for the review?
categories_or_examples: Examples: 'Hybrid of DTDG with continuous-time edges'; 'Unusual node typing'; 'Possibly should be excluded — graph-as-feature, not graph-as-data-structure'
